title: What Do Over 3,000 Bank Runs Teach Us About Banking Crises? - Liberty Street Economics
gist: Using a new database of 3,000+ historical U.S. bank runs (1863-1934), researchers find that weak bank fundamentals — not self-fulfilling panic dynamics — are the necessary condition for runs to cause failures and for failures to inflict real economic damage on local manufacturing, lending, and deposits.

themes.theme: Fundamentals as the Trigger for Runs

insights.claim: Banks in the weakest decile of fundamentals (capitalization, profitability, and reliance on cheap core deposits vs. expensive noncore funding) face nearly 100 basis points probability of a run in a given year, versus only about 25 basis points for banks with the strongest fundamentals — roughly a 4x gap.
insights.speaker: Sergio Correia, Stephan Luck, and Emil Verner
entities: Office of the Comptroller of the Currency
insights.so_what: A simple, replicable bank-health proxy (capitalization, profitability, funding mix) meaningfully predicts run risk — a template for screening bank credit/equity exposure by fundamentals rather than assuming panics are random.
insights.quote: A bank in the lowest decile of fundamentals has a nearly 100 basis point probability of being exposed to a run in a given year, whereas the probability is only about 25 basis points for banks with strong observable fundamentals.
insights.locator: A bank in the lowest decile of fundamentals

insights.claim: Runs on banks with strong fundamentals do occur, but they are comparatively rare and the absolute probability of a run even among weak banks remains low in any given year.
insights.speaker: Sergio Correia, Stephan Luck, and Emil Verner
insights.so_what: Undercuts the narrative that any bank, however sound, is equally vulnerable to a panic — fundamentals-based risk differentiation still matters even in run-prone systems.
insights.locator: Thus, runs on strong banks are less common

insights.claim: The historical U.S. banking system (1863-1934) is an unusually clean laboratory for studying runs because runs were far more common than today, a difference the authors attribute to modern government interventions such as deposit insurance and lender-of-last-resort facilities that alter depositor behavior.
insights.speaker: Sergio Correia, Stephan Luck, and Emil Verner
insights.so_what: Implies deposit insurance and central-bank backstops are the primary reason modern runs are rare, not improved bank fundamentals per se — a reminder that policy scaffolding, not just balance-sheet health, suppresses run frequency today.
insights.locator: This contrasts with the contemporary banking system, where runs are much less common

insights.claim: A bank subject to a run has a 38 percent probability of failing, dramatically higher than the unconditional annual bank failure probability of 0.85 percent — implying a run raises failure odds by roughly 40-45x relative to baseline.
insights.speaker: Sergio Correia, Stephan Luck, and Emil Verner
insights.so_what: Quantifies the run-to-failure conversion rate historically — useful as a base-rate anchor when assessing how alarmed to be once a run is confirmed at a given institution.
insights.quote: We find that a bank has a 38 percent probability of failing if it is subject to a run. This is a high conditional probability of failure, given that the annual unconditional probability of bank failure is 0.85 percent.
insights.locator: We find that a bank has a 38 percent probability of failing

insights.claim: More historical runs ended without bank failure than with it, meaning a run is not a death sentence for the institution.
insights.speaker: Sergio Correia, Stephan Luck, and Emil Verner
insights.so_what: Challenges the reflexive assumption that a visible run implies imminent collapse — survival is the more common outcome, conditional on solvency.
insights.quote: There are more runs without bank failure than runs with failure, highlighting a key novel insight from our approach.
insights.locator: There are more runs without bank failure

insights.claim: Conditional on experiencing a run, a bank in the weakest fundamentals decile has a 63 percent probability of failing, while a bank in the strongest fundamentals decile essentially never fails when run on.
insights.speaker: Sergio Correia, Stephan Luck, and Emil Verner
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete stress-test heuristic: fundamentals decile is the dominant determinant of run outcome, so due diligence on capitalization/funding mix should drive risk-weighting far more than the mere presence of a run.
insights.quote: conditional on a run, a bank with very weak fundamentals... has a 63 percent probability of failing. In contrast, the strongest banks... essentially never fail when subject to a run.
insights.locator: In our paper, we estimate that conditional on a run

insights.claim: Newspaper accounts identify seven recurring bank responses to runs: accommodating withdrawals while signaling strength (e.g., a publicized 'truckload of cash'), injecting additional owner equity, borrowing from other banks, partially suspending convertibility via 30/60-day notice rules on savings deposits, fully suspending convertibility in extreme cases, undergoing solvency examinations by state/federal supervisors or clearinghouses, and being closed if deemed insolvent.
insights.speaker: Sergio Correia, Stephan Luck, and Emil Verner
insights.so_what: A concrete taxonomy of liquidity-defense tools that historically severed illiquidity from insolvency — a checklist for evaluating whether a modern institution under stress is deploying credible defenses versus merely delaying failure.
insights.locator: We identify seven common responses discussed in newspapers

insights.claim: Clearinghouses are explicitly mentioned as intervening (via loans, examinations, or other measures) in only about 10 percent of historical run episodes, despite their reputation as a central stabilizing mechanism of the era.
insights.speaker: Sergio Correia, Stephan Luck, and Emil Verner
insights.so_what: Suggests most surviving banks relied on self-help measures (equity injections, suspension) rather than external institutional backstops — a nuance for anyone modeling systemic safety nets as the primary line of defense.
insights.quote: in about 10 percent of runs, newspapers explicitly mention the involvement of clearinghouses, either through the provision of a loan, examination, or other measure.
insights.locator: in about 10 percent of runs, newspapers explicitly mention

insights.claim: Using Bradstreet's weekly local economic activity data from 1917-1935, the authors find runs that end in bank failure predict a manufacturing activity decline of over 5 percent within the following eighteen months, while runs that do not end in failure show no significant decline in local manufacturing activity.
insights.speaker: Sergio Correia, Stephan Luck, and Emil Verner
insights.so_what: Localized real-economy damage tracks bank solvency outcomes, not the mere occurrence of a run — a filter for distinguishing which regional banking stress episodes are likely to spill into local credit and employment versus which are contained.
insights.quote: Runs without failure do not result in significantly lower manufacturing activity within the next eighteen months. In contrast, runs with failure predict a decline in manufacturing activity of over 5 percent.
insights.locator: Runs without failure do not result in significantly lower manufacturing

insights.claim: The evidence argues against the view that runs originating as pure liquidity events (i.e., on fundamentally sound banks) can by themselves generate severe disruptions in local credit markets and real economic activity.
insights.speaker: Sergio Correia, Stephan Luck, and Emil Verner
insights.so_what: Investors and policymakers assessing contagion risk from a bank run should weight the target bank's solvency far more heavily than the run's mere existence when forecasting spillover to the local economy.
insights.locator: This evidence argues against the view that runs

themes.theme: Reframing Crisis Theory: Trigger vs. Root Cause

insights.claim: The authors' overall findings lend little support to the theory that small shocks alone can spark self-fulfilling banking panics severe enough to cause major economic downturns, tempering a long-standing view in banking-crisis literature.
insights.speaker: Sergio Correia, Stephan Luck, and Emil Verner
insights.so_what: Pushes back against pure-panic/multiple-equilibria models of financial crises, implying policy and market attention should prioritize monitoring bank solvency indicators over trying to prevent panics in isolation.
insights.quote: Our findings lend little support to the view that small shocks by themselves can result in widespread banking panics that cause major economic downturns.
insights.locator: Our findings lend little support to the view that small shocks

insights.claim: The authors conclude runs should be understood as a trigger for bank failures and crises, but that insolvent (weak-fundamental) banks are the necessary condition for that trigger to devastate the banking system and broader economy.
insights.speaker: Sergio Correia, Stephan Luck, and Emil Verner
insights.so_what: A clean mental model for crisis-watching: treat visible runs as an alarm bell that should redirect attention to underlying solvency data, not as the causal mechanism itself — solvency screens remain the higher-value signal.
insights.quote: Runs should thus be seen as a trigger for bank failures and crises, but insolvent banks are necessary for this trigger to devastate the banking system and the economy.
insights.locator: Runs should thus be seen as a trigger for bank failures

top_entities: Federal Reserve Bank of New York
Office of the Comptroller of the Currency
